steps.caption: Visited both prevents cycles and doubles as the set of reachable nodes.

steps.q: Why does a graph traversal need a visited set when a tree traversal doesn't?
choices: Graphs can contain cycles, so without visited you'd revisit nodes and loop forever
Graphs are always larger than trees
Visited makes the traversal O(1)
Trees don't have edges
steps.explain: A tree has no cycles, so recursion terminates naturally. A graph may loop back on itself; the visited set is what stops infinite revisiting.
steps.nudge: What happens if A points to B and B points back to A and you never record where you've been?

md: ### LeetCode worked: Number of Islands

Given a grid of '1' (land) and '0' (water), count the islands — groups of horizontally/vertically connected land. Walk every cell; when you hit unvisited land, that's a new island, so bump the count and **flood fill** from it (DFS/BFS marking all connected land as visited) so you don't count the same island twice.
Each cell is visited a constant number of times — **O(rows × cols)**.

steps.title: number of islands (flood fill)
steps.caption: One flood fill per island sinks all of its land so it's counted once.

steps.title: Implement reachable
prompt: Given an adjacency map `graph` and a `start` node, return the set of all nodes reachable from `start` (including start). Use a visited set. Fill in the loop: for each neighbor `nbr` of `node`, if it's not in `visited`, add it and push it onto the stack.

checks.hint: Skip seen nodes: if nbr not in visited:.
Mark it seen: visited.add(nbr).
Queue it to explore: stack.append(nbr).

steps.success: Check-then-mark-then-push — the core of every graph traversal. O(nodes + edges).

md: ## Always grab the smallest (or largest) cheaply

A **priority queue** hands you the highest-priority item next, not the oldest. A **heap** is the data structure that implements it: it keeps the min (or max) at the top so you can peek it in O(1) and pop it in **O(log n)**, and push a new item in **O(log n)**.

It does *not* keep everything fully sorted — that would be more work. It only guarantees cheap access to the extreme, which is exactly what you need when you repeatedly want 'the next-most-urgent thing'.
### Python's heapq

Python ships `heapq`: a **min-heap** built on an ordinary list. `heapq.heappush(h, x)` and `heapq.heappop(h)` are O(log n); `h[0]` peeks the smallest in O(1). For a max-heap, push the negated values (or use a key).
Real uses: dispatch the *nearest* available walker (min-heap on distance), or run the *highest-priority* job first (push `(priority, job)` tuples).

steps.title: heapq in action
steps.source:
import heapq

# Dispatch: nearest walker first (min-heap on distance in meters)
walkers = []
heapq.heappush(walkers, (800, "Ana"))
heapq.heappush(walkers, (120, "Beto"))
heapq.heappush(walkers, (450, "Cira"))

print(heapq.heappop(walkers))   # (120, 'Beto') — closest
print(walkers[0])               # (450, 'Cira') — next closest, peeked

# nlargest / nsmallest for a quick top-k
print(heapq.nlargest(2, [5, 1, 8, 3, 9]))  # [9, 8]
steps.caption: Tuples compare element-by-element, so (distance, name) sorts by distance first.

steps.q: What does a heap give you that a fully-sorted list doesn't need to?
choices: O(log n) push and pop of the extreme element without keeping everything sorted
O(1) access to any element by index
Values in fully sorted order at all times
Constant-time insertion anywhere
steps.explain: A heap only guarantees the min (or max) is at the top. Push and pop are O(log n); it skips the extra work of a total ordering you don't need.
steps.nudge: You only ever look at the top of a heap — it doesn't waste effort sorting the rest.

md: ### LeetCode worked: Kth Largest Element
Find the kth largest value in `nums`. The quick way in Python is `heapq.nlargest(k, nums)[-1]` — the last of the top k is the kth largest.

The classic heap approach keeps a **min-heap of size k**: push each number, and whenever the heap exceeds k, pop the smallest. What survives is the k largest, and the heap's root is the kth largest — **O(n log k)** time, O(k) space, which beats sorting the whole list when k is small.

steps.title: kth largest, two ways
steps.caption: The size-k min-heap keeps only the k biggest; its root is the answer.

steps.title: Implement kth_largest
prompt: Return the kth largest element of `nums` using `heapq.nlargest`. It returns the top k in descending order, so the kth largest is the last one.
steps.starter:
import heapq

def kth_largest(nums, k):
    # your code here
    pass

checks.hint: Grab the top k: heapq.nlargest(k, nums).
The kth largest is the last of the top k: [-1].

steps.success: nlargest(k, nums)[-1] is the one-liner; the true heap approach is O(n log k) when you need to scale.

md: ## The hash map is the LeetCode swiss-army knife
Most 'medium' array/string problems come down to one of three hash-map moves:
- **Frequency counts**: how many times does each item appear? (`collections.Counter`)
- **Grouping by a canonical key**: bucket items that share some derived key. (`collections.defaultdict(list)`)
- **Seen-sets**: have I encountered this before? (a plain `set`, as in Two Sum and graph traversals)
### Counter and defaultdict

`Counter(iterable)` builds a dict of item -> count in one line. `defaultdict(list)` gives every missing key an empty list automatically, so you can `d[key].append(x)` without checking whether the key exists first. Both build in **O(n)**.
> Reaching for `if key in d: ... else: d[key] = ...` is the tell that a defaultdict would be cleaner.

steps.title: the three moves
steps.source:
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

# Frequency counts
walks = ["short", "long", "short", "short", "long"]
print(Counter(walks))          # Counter({'short': 3, 'long': 2})

# Grouping by a canonical key (first letter)
dogs = ["Rex", "Bella", "Rocky", "Bo"]
by_letter = defaultdict(list)
for name in dogs:
    by_letter[name[0]].append(name)
print(dict(by_letter))         # {'R': ['Rex', 'Rocky'], 'B': ['Bella', 'Bo']}

# Seen-set
seen = set()
print("Rex" in seen)           # False
steps.caption: Counter for counts, defaultdict(list) for buckets, set for membership.

steps.q: You want to group values under keys, appending as you go. What's the cleanest tool?
choices: collections.defaultdict(list) — a missing key auto-creates an empty list to append to
A Counter
A sorted list of tuples
A set
steps.explain: defaultdict(list) removes the 'is this key here yet?' check — d[key].append(x) just works. Counter is for counts; a set is for membership.
steps.nudge: You need each key to start life as an empty list without you writing the check.

md: ### LeetCode worked: Group Anagrams

Group words that are anagrams of each other (same letters, reordered): `['eat','tea','tan','ate']` -> `[['eat','tea','ate'], ['tan']]`. The insight: two words are anagrams iff their **sorted letters** are identical. So the canonical key is `''.join(sorted(word))`, and you bucket words under that key with a defaultdict.
Sorting each word of length k is O(k log k); across n words that's **O(n · k log k)**.

steps.title: group_anagrams
steps.caption: Anagrams collapse to the same sorted-letters key, so they land in the same bucket.

steps.title: Implement group_anagrams
prompt: Loop over `words`. Build `key` from the sorted letters with `"".join(sorted(word))`, append the word to `groups[key]`, and return `list(groups.values())`.

checks.hint: Canonical key: key = "".join(sorted(word)).
Bucket it: groups[key].append(word).
Return the buckets: list(groups.values()).

steps.success: Group-by-canonical-key is the capstone pattern: turn 'are these the same underneath?' into a dict lookup. O(n · k log k).

md: ## One shared instance for the whole app

**Singleton** guarantees a class has exactly one instance and gives everyone a single point of access to it. The classic use is a shared resource: one app `Config`, one logger, one Stripe client that every part of PawWalk talks to.

Here is the honest part you rarely hear: in Python you almost never need the textbook singleton. A plain **module** is already a singleton — imported once, cached in `sys.modules`, shared everywhere. A module-level `config = Config()` gives you a shared instance with zero ceremony.
### When you do want the class to enforce it

If you truly want `Config()` to always hand back the *same* object no matter who calls it, override `__new__` to cache the instance on the class. `__new__` runs before `__init__` and decides which object you get back.
> `functools.lru_cache` on a factory function is another clean trick — the first call builds it, every later call returns the cached one.

steps.title: singleton via __new__
steps.caption: __new__ caches the one instance on the class; every call returns it.

steps.q: Why is a plain Python module often enough instead of a Singleton class?
choices: A module is imported once and cached in sys.modules, so a module-level object is already shared everywhere
Modules cannot hold state
Python forbids more than one instance of any class
Modules are faster than classes at runtime
steps.explain: Import caching makes a module a de-facto singleton; a module-level instance is shared app-wide with no boilerplate.
steps.nudge: Think about what happens the second time you `import config`.

steps.title: Make Config a singleton
prompt: Override `__new__` so every `Config()` returns the same cached instance. Store it on `cls._instance` and return it.

checks.hint: Guard: if cls._instance is None:
Build it once: cls._instance = super().__new__(cls).

mustNot.hint: You only need to override __new__ here.

steps.success: One cached instance, handed back to every caller — the singleton, done the Python way.

md: ## Let a method decide which object to build

**Factory Method** puts object creation behind a function so callers ask for *what* they want, not *how* it's made. When a booking update needs to go out, you don't want `if channel == ...` scattered everywhere — you want one `notifier_for(channel)` that returns the right notifier.
In Python this is just a function that returns an object. No abstract creator class required — a dict or an `if`/`elif` does the job.

steps.title: notifier_for
steps.caption: One place knows the mapping; callers just ask by channel string.

steps.q: What does the factory method buy you here?
choices: Callers depend on a channel string, not on the concrete notifier classes — creation logic lives in one place
It makes the notifiers run faster
It removes the need for the notifier classes entirely
It guarantees only one notifier ever exists
steps.explain: The knowledge of which class maps to which channel is centralized; adding a new channel touches one function.
steps.nudge: Where does the `if channel == ...` logic live now?

steps.title: Write notifier_for
prompt: Return an EmailNotifier for "email", else an SmsNotifier. Return an *instance*, not the class.

checks.hint: Branch on the channel: if channel == "email":.
Return an instance: return EmailNotifier().
Default to return SmsNotifier().

mustNot.hint: Return the instance EmailNotifier(), not the class.

steps.success: A tiny factory: ask by channel, get the matching notifier back.

md: ## Build a whole *family* of matched objects

Where Factory Method makes one product, **Abstract Factory** makes a *family* of related products that must go together. For payments you need a matched pair: a **charger** and a **refunder**. A Stripe charger must be paired with a Stripe refunder — never a PayPal one.
The abstract factory hands you a coherent set. Pick Stripe once and you get the whole Stripe family; you can't accidentally mix providers.

steps.title: payment provider families
steps.caption: One choice yields a matched charge/refund pair from the same provider.

steps.q: How does Abstract Factory differ from a plain Factory Method?
choices: It produces a family of related objects that belong together, not a single product
It can only ever return one object
It requires inheritance in Python
It is the same thing under a different name
steps.explain: The point is coherence across a set: pick the provider once and every related object comes from that same family.
steps.nudge: How many related methods does the returned object expose?

steps.title: Return a matched provider
prompt: Complete `provider_for`: return a StripeProvider for "stripe", otherwise a PaypalProvider. Both expose charge and refund.

checks.hint: Branch: if name == "stripe":.
Return StripeProvider().
Default to PaypalProvider().

mustNot.hint: Just return the provider; the caller picks charge vs refund.

steps.success: One call, a whole coherent provider family — charger and refunder that always match.

md: ## Assemble a complex object step by step

**Builder** separates constructing a complicated object from its final shape, so you build it in readable steps instead of one monster constructor. A booking search query has filters, a sort, a page — passing all of that positionally is a nightmare.
In Python the idiomatic builder is a class whose methods each return `self`, so calls chain fluently, ending in `.build()` that returns the assembled dict.

steps.title: QueryBuilder
steps.caption: Each step returns self, so the calls read like a sentence.

steps.q: Why does each builder method `return self`?
choices: So calls can be chained fluently: builder.where(...).sort_by(...).build()
Because Python requires every method to return something
To make a copy of the builder each time
It has no effect; you could drop it
steps.explain: Returning self is what makes the fluent chain possible — each call hands the same builder back for the next step.
steps.nudge: What would `.where(...).sort_by(...)` break if where returned None?

steps.title: Chainable where + build
prompt: Give QueryBuilder a `where(**kw)` that updates `self._filters` and returns self, plus `build()` returning `self._filters`.

checks.hint: Accumulate: self._filters.update(kw).
Make it chainable: return self.
build() returns the assembled self._filters.

mustNot.hint: Return the accumulated filters, not the latest kwargs.

steps.success: A fluent builder: chain .where(...) as many times as you like, then .build().

md: ## Make new objects by cloning an existing one

**Prototype** creates a new object by copying a fully-configured existing one, instead of building it from scratch. When a recurring booking template already has the walker, dog, and price set, cloning it and tweaking just the date is far cheaper than reassembling everything.
Python hands you this for free: `copy.copy` for a shallow copy, `copy.deepcopy` when nested objects must be copied too. No `clone()` method to write.

steps.title: copy a template
steps.source:
import copy

class BookingTemplate:
    def __init__(self, dog, price_cents, date):
        self.dog = dog
        self.price_cents = price_cents
        self.date = date

template = BookingTemplate("Rex", 2500, "2026-07-10")
monday = copy.copy(template)
monday.date = "2026-07-13"

print(template.date)   # 2026-07-10 — original untouched
print(monday.date)     # 2026-07-13
steps.caption: copy.copy clones the template; changing the copy leaves the original intact.

steps.q: When would you reach for copy.deepcopy over copy.copy?
choices: When the object holds nested mutable objects you also need to copy, not share
Always — deepcopy is strictly better
Only for immutable objects
When you want the copy to share state with the original
steps.explain: Shallow copy shares the inner objects; deepcopy recursively copies them so the clone is fully independent.
steps.nudge: What happens to a nested list under a shallow copy if you mutate it?

steps.title: Clone and tweak
prompt: Make a shallow copy of `template` with `copy.copy`, assign it to `clone`, and set `clone.date` to "2026-07-20".
steps.starter:
import copy
clone = None
# your code here
steps.solution:
import copy
clone = copy.copy(template)
clone.date = "2026-07-20"

checks.hint: Clone it: clone = copy.copy(template).
Tweak the copy: clone.date = "2026-07-20".

mustNot.hint: Change the clone's date, not the template's.

steps.success: Cloned the template and changed one field — the original stays pristine.

md: ## Make a foreign interface fit the one you expect

**Adapter** wraps an object with an incompatible interface so it looks like the interface your code already uses. A third-party geocoder exposes `lat_lng(address)`, but the rest of PawWalk calls `geocoder.coordinates(address)`. Rather than rewrite every caller, you wrap the foreign object.
The adapter holds the foreign object and translates the call — a thin layer of delegation.

steps.title: GeocoderAdapter
steps.caption: coordinates() is the interface we want; it delegates to the foreign lat_lng().

steps.q: What problem does the Adapter solve?
choices: It lets code that expects coordinates() use an object that only offers lat_lng(), without changing either side
It speeds up the foreign geocoder
It removes the dependency on the foreign library
It caches geocoding results
steps.explain: Adapter bridges a name/shape mismatch so incompatible interfaces work together untouched.
steps.nudge: Which method name does your code call, and which does the library offer?

steps.title: Delegate coordinates -> lat_lng
prompt: Complete `coordinates`: call the foreign object's `lat_lng(address)` and return its result.

checks.hint: Delegate: return self._foreign.lat_lng(address).

mustNot.hint: The adapter exposes coordinates(); it doesn't redefine lat_lng.

steps.success: The adapter speaks your interface and quietly forwards to the foreign one.

md: ## Split abstraction from implementation

**Bridge** separates a thing (an abstraction) from *how* it's carried out (an implementation) so the two can vary independently. A `Notification` is the abstraction — it has a message and a recipient. The `Transport` (SMS, email) is the implementation. Either axis can grow without touching the other.
Instead of subclassing `Notification` into `SmsNotification`, `EmailNotification`, you *inject* the transport. New transports and new notification types no longer multiply into a combinatorial mess.

steps.title: Notification + Transport
steps.caption: The Notification delegates delivery to whichever transport you injected.

steps.q: What does the Bridge let you vary independently?
choices: The notification kinds and the transports — add either without a class explosion of every combination
Only the message text
Nothing; it's the same as inheritance
The recipient's address format
steps.explain: Abstraction and implementation live on separate axes; you combine them at runtime instead of pre-baking every pairing into a subclass.
steps.nudge: How many subclasses would you need for 3 notification types x 3 transports without a bridge?

steps.title: Delegate send to transport
prompt: Complete `send`: call the injected transport's `deliver(to, body)` and return it.

checks.hint: Delegate: return self._transport.deliver(to, body).

mustNot.hint: Notification delegates to the transport; it doesn't implement deliver.

steps.success: The abstraction and the transport now vary on their own axes — that's the bridge.

md: ## Treat parts and wholes uniformly

**Composite** lets you build a tree where a single item and a group of items share the same interface, so client code can call `total_price()` on either without checking which it is. A walk package can hold plain items *and* sub-packages; a group's price is just the sum of its children.
The trick: both the leaf and the group answer the same method. Recursion falls out naturally — a group asks each child for its price.

steps.title: packages and items
steps.caption: A Package sums its children — leaves and sub-packages answer total_price the same way.

steps.q: Why can total_price() be called the same way on an Item and a Package?
choices: Both expose the same method; a Package recurses over its children while a leaf returns its own price
Packages secretly convert themselves into items first
Python flattens the tree automatically
Only Packages have total_price; Items borrow it
steps.explain: Uniform interface + recursion: the client never branches on leaf-vs-group; the group sums, the leaf returns.
steps.nudge: What does Package.total_price call on each child?

steps.title: Sum the children
prompt: Complete `Package.total_price`: sum `total_price()` over each child in `self.children`.

checks.hint: Recurse: return sum(c.total_price() for c in self.children).

mustNot.hint: A Package has no own price; it sums its children.

steps.success: A group that totals its children — parts and wholes, handled uniformly.

md: ## Wrap an object to add behavior without changing it

**Decorator** wraps an object in another object that shares its interface and adds behavior on top. Start with a base walk price; wrap it to add a GPS surcharge; wrap *that* to add insurance. Each layer adds a bit and forwards the rest — the base class never changes.
This is the structural, object-wrapping Decorator — not the same as Python's `@decorator` syntax (which is a function-wrapping cousin of the same idea).

steps.title: price decorators
steps.caption: Each Surcharge wraps the last and adds its own on top of the wrapped total.

steps.q: What makes this a Decorator and not just a subclass?
choices: It wraps an existing object at runtime and shares its interface, so you can stack layers in any combination
It changes the BasePrice class in place
It requires inheritance from BasePrice
It can only add exactly one surcharge
steps.explain: Composition over inheritance: wrap and forward, stacking behaviors dynamically without touching the wrapped class.
steps.nudge: How many surcharges did the example stack, and did BasePrice change?

steps.title: Add a surcharge to the wrapped total
prompt: Complete `Surcharge.total`: return the wrapped object's total plus `self._extra`.

checks.hint: Forward and add: return self._wrapped.total() + self._extra.

mustNot.hint: Include the wrapped total, not just the surcharge.

steps.success: A wrapper that adds on top of whatever it wraps — stack as many as you like.

md: ## One simple door over a messy subsystem

**Facade** gives a small, friendly entry point over a tangle of subsystems. Booking a walk really means: price it, charge the card, notify the owner. Callers shouldn't juggle three objects and their order — they call `BookingService.book()` and it orchestrates the rest.
The facade doesn't add new behavior; it *coordinates* existing pieces so the common path is one call.

steps.title: BookingService facade
steps.caption: One call hides pricing + payment + notification behind a single method.

steps.q: What does a Facade add over calling the subsystems directly?
choices: A simple, single entry point that coordinates the subsystems in the right order
New business logic the subsystems don't have
Faster execution of each subsystem
It replaces the subsystems entirely
steps.explain: The facade is about simplicity and coordination — callers get one method instead of orchestrating parts.
steps.nudge: Does book() invent new logic, or sequence existing objects?

steps.title: Orchestrate the subsystems
prompt: Complete `book`: quote the price into `cents`, charge it, then return `cents`.

checks.hint: Quote first: cents = self.pricing.quote(dog).
Then charge: self.payments.charge(cents).
Return the total: return cents.

mustNot.hint: The facade calls quote; it doesn't redefine it.

steps.success: One method, the whole booking flow — that's the facade's whole job.

md: ## Share immutable objects to save memory

**Flyweight** shares one copy of an object across many owners instead of duplicating it. Thousands of `Dog` instances each carry a breed — but there are only ~200 breeds, and a breed is immutable. Interning them means every Golden Retriever points at the *same* `Breed` object.
The realization in Python is a factory with a cache dict: same key, same instance returned. Identity (`is`) proves the sharing.

steps.title: breed factory with a cache
steps.caption: The cache guarantees one instance per breed name, shared by every dog.

steps.q: What must be true of a Flyweight object for sharing to be safe?
choices: It must be immutable (or its shared state must be), so no owner can mutate what everyone shares
It must be a subclass of dict
It must be recreated on every call
It must hold each dog's unique data
steps.explain: Shared state has to be intrinsic and immutable; per-owner (extrinsic) data stays outside the flyweight.
steps.nudge: What breaks if one dog could rename the shared Breed object?

steps.title: Cache one instance per name
prompt: Complete `breed`: if `name` isn't in `_breeds`, store `Breed(name)` there; then return `_breeds[name]`.

checks.hint: Guard: if name not in _breeds:.
Cache it: _breeds[name] = Breed(name).

mustNot.hint: Return the cached instance, not a fresh one.

steps.success: Repeated calls for the same breed hand back the exact same object — memory saved.

md: ## A stand-in that controls access to the real object

**Proxy** is an object that fronts another object with the same interface, adding control: lazy loading, access checks, rate limiting, caching. A walker's photo is expensive to load — a proxy defers loading until the first read, then delegates to the real subject.
Same interface as the real thing, but the proxy decides *when* and *whether* the real work happens.

steps.title: lazy photo proxy
steps.caption: Nothing loads until data() is first called — the proxy gates the real object.

steps.q: What is the proxy doing that a direct reference wouldn't?
choices: Controlling access — here deferring the expensive load until the object is actually needed
Changing the photo's contents
Making the photo load faster
Removing the RealPhoto class
steps.explain: A proxy shares the subject's interface but interposes control: lazy init, auth, throttling, caching.
steps.nudge: When does RealPhoto actually get constructed?

steps.title: Load on first use
prompt: Complete `data`: if `self._real` is None, set it to `RealPhoto(self._path)`; then return `self._real.data`.

checks.hint: Guard: if self._real is None:.
Load it: self._real = RealPhoto(self._path).
Delegate: return self._real.data.

mustNot.hint: Cache the real object in self._real, don't rebuild it each call.

steps.success: The real photo loads only when first read — the proxy controlled access.

md: ## Pass a request down a line of handlers

**Chain of Responsibility** sends a request along a sequence of handlers; each either handles it or passes it on. A booking must clear a pipeline: availability, then payment, then policy. The *first* failing check stops the chain and reports why.
In Python the whole pattern often collapses to a list of callables and a loop — no linked handler objects needed.

steps.title: validation pipeline
steps.caption: Each handler returns None to pass, or an error string to halt the chain.

steps.q: How is Chain of Responsibility usually realized in idiomatic Python?
choices: As a list of callables the request passes through, stopping at the first that rejects it
As a single giant if/elif block
As a subclass per handler with mandatory inheritance
As a global variable holding the result
steps.explain: First-class functions make the chain a plain list of callables — no handler base class required.
steps.nudge: What does the loop do the moment a handler returns an error?

steps.title: Stop at the first failure
prompt: Complete `run_chain`: call each handler on `req`; if it returns a truthy error, return that error. If none fail, return "ok".

checks.hint: Run it: error = handler(req).
Check it: if error:.
Bail early: return error.

mustNot.hint: Return "ok" only after the loop, once all handlers pass.

steps.success: The request flows through handlers and halts at the first rejection.

md: ## Package a request as an object

**Command** turns a request into a standalone object with an `execute` method, so you can queue it, log it, retry it, or undo it. Background jobs are the perfect fit: each job *is* a command; a runner just calls each one.
In Python any callable is already a command — a function, a lambda, or an object with `__call__`. The pattern earns its keep when the command also needs to carry state or support undo.

steps.title: commands and a runner
steps.caption: Each command carries its own data and runs via __call__; the runner just calls each.

steps.q: Why package a request as a Command object instead of just calling a function?
choices: So the request can be stored, queued, logged, retried, or undone — it's a value you can pass around
Because functions can't take arguments
It makes the request run twice as fast
Python requires classes for all callbacks
steps.explain: Reifying the request as an object lets you defer, schedule, record, and reverse it — exactly what a job queue needs.
steps.nudge: What can you do with a command object that you can't do with an already-executed call?

steps.title: Run a list of commands
prompt: Complete `run_all`: call each command in `commands` and return the list of their results.
steps.solution:
def run_all(commands):
    return [cmd() for cmd in commands]

checks.hint: Run each: return [cmd() for cmd in commands].

mustNot.hint: Return the results of calling them, not the commands themselves.

steps.success: A runner that executes any list of commands — the heart of a job queue.

md: ## Evaluate sentences in a tiny language

**Interpreter** defines a grammar and an evaluator for a small domain language. Honestly? This is the rarest GoF pattern in everyday app code — if you need a real language you reach for a parser library, not hand-rolled Interpreter classes. But a *tiny* rule language shows the idea.
PawWalk scenario: a price-adjustment rule expressed as tokens like `("add", 500)`, `("sub", 200)`, evaluated against a starting total.

steps.title: evaluate price tokens
steps.caption: Each token is a tiny instruction; the interpreter folds them over the total.

steps.q: Why is Interpreter rare in ordinary application code?
choices: For anything beyond a trivial grammar you use a real parser/library, not hand-written Interpreter classes
Because Python can't evaluate expressions
Because it's the fastest pattern and thus overused
Because it's identical to Strategy
steps.explain: Real languages need real parsers; Interpreter only pays off for very small, stable, in-house grammars.
steps.nudge: What would you actually reach for to parse a non-trivial expression language?

steps.title: Fold the tokens
prompt: Complete `evaluate`: for each `(op, amount)`, add on "add" and subtract on "sub", then return the running `total`.

checks.hint: Handle add: if op == "add":.
Add it: total += amount.
Subtract on sub: total -= amount.

mustNot.hint: Return the accumulated total, not a single amount.

steps.success: A minimal interpreter — it folds a list of instructions into a result.

md: ## Step through a collection without exposing its guts

**Iterator** lets you traverse a collection's elements without revealing how it stores them. This is the pattern most fully absorbed into the language: Python's `for` loop *is* the iterator protocol. You define `__iter__` (often as a generator) and the whole ecosystem — `for`, `list()`, `sum()`, unpacking — just works.
A `Calendar` of bookings can decide its own order and yield entries; callers never touch the internal list.

steps.title: a custom iterable
steps.caption: Defining __iter__ as a generator makes the object work with any for-loop.

steps.q: What does defining __iter__ give a custom class?
choices: It becomes iterable — usable in for-loops, list(), sum(), and unpacking, with the internals hidden
It becomes a dict
It gains a length automatically
It can only be looped over once ever
steps.explain: The iterator protocol is built in; __iter__ is the single hook the whole language traverses through.
steps.nudge: Which language construct calls __iter__ under the hood?

steps.title: Yield the items
prompt: Complete `__iter__`: loop over `self._bookings` and `yield` each one.

checks.hint: Loop: for b in self._bookings:.
Yield each: yield b.

mustNot.hint: Yield the items to be an iterator, don't return the raw list.

steps.success: A custom iterable — the for-loop drives it and the internals stay hidden.

md: ## A hub so peers don't reference each other

**Mediator** centralizes communication in one object so participants talk to the hub, not to each other. A dispatch center coordinates the walker, the owner, and the GPS tracker: when one raises an event, the mediator decides who else hears it. The peers stay decoupled.
Without a mediator, every participant holds references to every other — an n-to-n tangle. With one, it's n-to-hub.

steps.title: dispatch mediator
steps.caption: notify routes an event from one peer to all the others through the hub.

steps.q: What coupling problem does the Mediator fix?
choices: It removes the n-to-n web of peers referencing each other — everyone talks to one hub instead
It makes each peer faster
It lets peers skip having a receive method
It merges all peers into one object
steps.explain: Centralizing interaction turns a combinatorial mesh of references into a single coordinating point.
steps.nudge: How many references does each peer need if they all go through the hub?

steps.title: Route to everyone but the sender
prompt: Complete `notify`: for each registered `(name, p)`, if `name` isn't the sender, append `p.receive(event)` to `out`; return `out`.

checks.hint: Skip the sender: if name != sender:.
Route it: out.append(p.receive(event)).

mustNot.hint: You route to everyone EXCEPT the sender.

steps.success: The hub fans one peer's event out to the rest — peers stay decoupled.

md: ## Capture state now, restore it later

**Memento** snapshots an object's internal state into a separate token so you can restore it later — without exposing how the object stores things. A booking draft can be saved before risky edits; if the user cancels, you put the saved state back.
The memento is just captured data (often a dict copy). `save_state()` produces it; `restore(memento)` writes it back.

steps.title: draft with undo
steps.source:
class BookingDraft:
    def __init__(self, dog, date):
        self.dog = dog
        self.date = date

    def save_state(self):
        return {"dog": self.dog, "date": self.date}   # the memento

    def restore(self, memento):
        self.dog = memento["dog"]
        self.date = memento["date"]

draft = BookingDraft("Rex", "2026-07-10")
snapshot = draft.save_state()
draft.date = "oops"
draft.restore(snapshot)
print(draft.date)   # 2026-07-10 — restored
steps.caption: save_state captures the fields; restore writes a saved snapshot back.

steps.q: What is the memento itself in this design?
choices: A captured copy of the object's state that can be handed back to restore()
A reference to the live object, so edits show through
A subclass of BookingDraft
A global variable holding all drafts
steps.explain: The memento is a self-contained snapshot; because it's a copy, later edits to the object don't corrupt it.
steps.nudge: If the memento were a live reference, would restoring undo your changes?

steps.title: Capture the state
prompt: Complete `save_state`: return a dict copying `self.dog` and `self.date`.

checks.hint: Snapshot both fields: return {"dog": self.dog, "date": self.date}.

mustNot.hint: Return a copy of the fields, not the live object.

steps.success: A snapshot you can stash and restore — undo, the GoF way.

md: ## Subscribers get notified when the subject changes

**Observer** lets a subject keep a list of interested parties and notify them all when something happens — without knowing who they are. When a walk's status changes, the owner's app, analytics, and push service all need to hear it. The subject just calls each subscriber.
In Python a subscriber is any callable, so the whole pattern is a list of callbacks plus a `notify` that calls each. (`from observer import ...`-style base classes are optional ceremony.)

steps.title: status subject
steps.caption: Subscribers are just callables; notify fans the event out to each.

steps.q: What does the subject know about its observers?
choices: Only that each is callable — it holds a list and calls them, staying decoupled from who they are
Their concrete classes and method names
Exactly one observer at a time
Nothing; observers poll the subject instead
steps.explain: The subject depends only on the callable contract, so you can add or remove observers freely.
steps.nudge: Does the subject import or name any specific subscriber class?

steps.title: Notify every subscriber
prompt: Complete `notify`: call each callback in `self._subscribers`, passing `status`.

checks.hint: Loop: for cb in self._subscribers:.
Call each: cb(status).

mustNot.hint: Call each subscriber, not the list itself.

steps.success: One status change, every subscriber notified — the observer loop.

md: ## Behavior changes with internal state, via legal transitions

**State** lets an object change its behavior when its internal state changes, moving between states only along allowed edges. A booking goes `requested -> confirmed -> in_progress -> completed`, or can be `cancelled` from some states — but you must never jump from `completed` back to `requested`.
The lazy, robust realization is a transitions map: `state -> set of allowed next states`. Any move not in the map raises.

steps.title: booking state machine
steps.caption: The map encodes every legal edge; illegal moves raise instead of corrupting state.

steps.q: Why drive the machine from an ALLOWED transitions map?
choices: It makes legal moves explicit and data-driven, so illegal transitions are rejected in one place
It runs faster than an if/elif chain
It lets any state reach any other state
It removes the need to store the current status
steps.explain: A declarative map centralizes the rules; the transition logic is one guard, and the edges are data you can audit.
steps.nudge: Where is the rule that completed can't go anywhere?

steps.title: Guard the transition
prompt: Complete `transition`: if `to` isn't in `ALLOWED[self.status]`, raise ValueError; otherwise set `self.status = to`.

checks.hint: Guard: if to not in ALLOWED[self.status]:.
Reject illegal moves: raise ValueError(...).
On success: self.status = to.

mustNot.hint: Check legality BEFORE assigning the new status.

steps.success: Only legal transitions succeed; everything else raises — a safe state machine.

md: ## Swap interchangeable algorithms at runtime

**Strategy** captures a family of algorithms behind one interface so you can pick which to use at runtime. Pricing has several strategies — flat, surge, member-discount — and the right one depends on the booking. The calculator doesn't care which; it just applies the strategy it's given.
Say it plainly: in Python a strategy is almost always **just a callable** — a function or lambda you pass in. No Strategy base class, no subclass per algorithm.

steps.title: pricing strategies as callables
steps.caption: The strategy is a plain function; swap it to change the algorithm.

steps.q: What's the idiomatic Python realization of Strategy?
choices: Pass a callable (function or lambda) — no base class or per-algorithm subclass needed
One giant class with an if/elif for every algorithm
A subclass of PriceCalculator per pricing rule
A global flag that the calculator reads
steps.explain: First-class functions ARE the strategy object; injecting a callable is the whole pattern.
steps.nudge: What type is `surge` in the example — a class or a function?

steps.title: Apply the strategy
prompt: Complete `price`: apply `self._strategy` to `base` and return the result.

checks.hint: Apply it: return self._strategy(base).

mustNot.hint: Don't branch on algorithms — just call the injected strategy.

steps.success: One line applies whatever strategy you injected — algorithms swapped at runtime.

md: ## Base defines the skeleton; subclasses fill in steps

**Template Method** puts the overall algorithm in a base method that calls smaller steps, letting subclasses override individual steps without changing the sequence. A report generator's `run()` always does header, then body, then footer — but each report type supplies its own `body()`.
This one genuinely benefits from inheritance: the base fixes the order; subclasses customize the variable parts.

steps.title: report sections
steps.caption: run() fixes the header/body/footer order; the subclass overrides only body.

steps.q: Which part does the subclass control in a Template Method?
choices: Individual steps it overrides — the base method keeps the overall sequence fixed
The order the steps run in
Whether run() is called at all
Nothing; the base does everything
steps.explain: The invariant algorithm lives in the base; subclasses vary only the designated steps.
steps.nudge: Does WalksReport change when header runs, or just what body returns?

steps.title: Override the body step
prompt: Subclass ReportSection as WalksReport and override `body` to return "12 walks completed".

checks.hint: Subclass it: class WalksReport(ReportSection):.
Override the step: def body(self):.
Return "12 walks completed".

mustNot.hint: Don't override run — only the body step.

steps.success: The base skeleton stays fixed; your subclass supplied just the body.

md: ## Add operations over a fixed structure via double dispatch

**Visitor** lets you add new operations to a set of object types *without* modifying them, by having each element `accept(visitor)` and call back `visitor.visit_X(self)`. That two-hop callback is **double dispatch** — the method that runs depends on both the element type and the visitor type.

Be honest: Visitor is clunky in dynamic languages. In Python you'd usually just write a function that does a `match`/`isinstance` on the item type — no accept methods, no double dispatch. Learn it, but reach for pattern matching first.

steps.title: line items and visitors
steps.caption: accept calls back into visit_item, so the item type and visitor type together pick the code.

steps.q: Why is Visitor often replaced by pattern matching in Python?
choices: A match/isinstance on the item type does the same dispatch without accept methods and double-dispatch boilerplate
Because Python lacks classes
Because Visitor is faster and thus unnecessary
Because double dispatch is impossible in Python
steps.explain: Dynamic dispatch on type is trivial with match/isinstance, so the accept/visit ceremony rarely pays off.
steps.nudge: What could you write instead of accept + visit_item to branch on the item's type?

steps.title: Wire up accept
prompt: Complete `Item.accept`: call `visitor.visit_item(self)` and return it.

checks.hint: Double dispatch: return visitor.visit_item(self).

mustNot.hint: The item defines accept; the visitor defines visit_item.

steps.success: accept calls back into the visitor — double dispatch wired, ceremony and all.
